name: anyhow (Flexible Error Handling for Rust)

performance.notes: Error creation has heap allocation; negligible in practice

agent_readiness.notes: Flexible error handling for Rust applications. Result<T, anyhow::Error> as return type. ? operator works on any error type. anyhow!("message"), bail!("message"), ensure!(condition, "message"). Context with .context("doing X") or .with_context(). Error chain with {:?} debug output. Use in application code; use thiserror for library code. Pair: anyhow (apps) + thiserror (libraries). Zero boilerplate error handling. By David Tolnay (also: serde, syn, quote, thiserror).

gotchas: Not for library code (use thiserror for typed errors in libraries)
Hides error type information (good for apps, bad for libraries)
anyhow::Error is not Send+Sync on all platforms by default
best_when: Application code needing quick ? propagation across error types
CLIs, scripts, and servers where error type doesn't need to be precise
Rapid prototyping in Rust before adding typed errors
Pairing with thiserror: anyhow in main.rs, thiserror in lib.rs
avoid_when: Library code (use thiserror for typed error enums callers can match on)
Code where callers need to handle specific error variants
summary: anyhow provides flexible error handling for Rust applications. Result<T, anyhow::Error> with ? across all error types. bail!, ensure!, .context() for error messages. Error chains with debug formatting. Use in application code; use thiserror for libraries. The canonical pairing: anyhow (app entry points) + thiserror (library error types). Zero boilerplate error propagation.
